moment_text: I was diagnosed with breast breast cancer when I was 42, and my mom was a two time breast cancer survivor. And when I say that she was diagnosed with breast cancer in one breast. Treated and years later diagnosed again in the second breast. And so when I got diagnosed, I felt like I was somebody who was pretty well informed around what breast cancer was, um, supported and educated in the field of women's health. I was also a midwife as a career, and so, um. When I got diagnosed it, I wasn't expecting it to happen so young. I know it's never a good time, but I still felt like I'm only 42. My kid, I still have kids to get through school. I not now. Um, you know, I, I can't say I was surprised by the diagnosis. I was just surprised it came so soon and I wasn't ready. Um, and then further compounded. By what I learned around dense breast tissue really threw me for a loop. And I thought, if somebody like me doesn't know any of this, given my family history and my education, what is everybody else going through that doesn't have these resources and this information? And again, this was. You know, back in 2014 we weren't even having these conversations and so that was really a driving force behind the work that I was doing. I think I found maybe one other organization or one other person even talking around about dense breast tissue back then. So it's really been a long time coming that we make some of these changes and really amplify these conversations.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with barrier signals (lack_of_information, education_gap) related to a systemic knowledge gap in women's health.

moment_text: You know, I feel like I was a little bit lucky. I call myself halfway lucky in that in California. So it's important we talk about what state we're in as well, because. The laws have changed and, but back then it was only a state by state around whether we notified women. Um, I was told in person by a radiologist that I had dense breast tissue, but I was also told, um, that it's normal that, um, insurance doesn't cover it. And that. They recommended, maybe I might wanna get an ultrasound, but that was gonna cost another $300 and then it was gonna probably cost more money out of pocket to have a radiologist look at it and interpret those results. And. I was a single mom at the time. I was working, I wasn't practicing midwifery at this point as a divorced single mom. I was, I was in a field, you know, working nine to five, hustling so hard, usually over time just to keep the lights on, food on the table and the electricity bill paid. And I, I just thought. Well, okay, if you're telling me this is normal, then I am not gonna spend the money because I don't have it. First of all, there's, I just don't have it. Um, it's that, or, you know, paying for my daughter's, you know, school or soccer or food or what have you. It was a literal choice I had to make. And, um, at that point, I wasn't given enough information to make me feel like it was an important enough thing to go do to. To even ask for more help, ask for more funding, uh, like there was no reason for me to continue to pursue that except for that of my family history. And something didn't quite sit right in my stomach about it. Um, so it was a little bit heartbreaking. I was giving just enough information to make it really difficult to know what to do about it, but not enough. To actually be able to take action and go forward, um, on next steps. And so I found that incredibly frustrating. And looking back on what I know now, I definitely would've handled that differently. But with the information I had at the time, that's all I could do. Um, and so I know women today are still making those choices between do I go for that follow up? Ultrasound or MRI or do I put food on the table? And, um, these are terrible choices that no woman should have to be making when she's trying to care for her family or, you know, be an active participant in her own life.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong barrier signals (cost_constraint, symptoms_normalized_without_context) demonstrating a direct access and communication failure in healthcare.

moment_text: Um, and so fast forward, um. Maybe a year and a half later, um, I, that that feeling in my stomach was just getting louder and louder and louder of like, something's not right. I really should probably make an effort. I just knew something in my gut, something was off and I really needed to go back and, and do whatever I had to, to get that ultrasound. And then that's when I was diagnosed with breast cancer.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with clear agency signals (self_advocacy) and barrier signals (delayed_diagnosis), highlighting the role of patient intuition in overcoming system delays.

moment_text: Um, well, I learned a little bit about the system at this time and how easy it is to fall through the cracks. Um, I had wanted to just go back for an ultrasound. I didn't really feel like I wanted, nor needed another mammogram, but, um, they insisted that I start with the mammogram again, like start the whole cycle over again. So this is just. Perpetuating the delay of getting this extra screening done. Um, and when I went back and tried to schedule, they said, no. We're gonna have to start with the, the mammogram again. We're gonna start from the beginning. Um. And then we can follow up with an ultrasound, which is was just their recommendation. The truth was I didn't actually know, um, except for what the, the radiologist had told me is that they were recommending an ultrasound. At this point, I didn't even know a breast MRI would be an option. So that's all that was recommended to me at this point.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with clear barrier signals (system_navigation_difficulty, time_constraint, lack_of_information) that critique a systemic process flaw.

moment_text: At this point though, my mammogram showed some changes from the one that I had had even a year and a half prior. And so that's what alerted them to then push me through to get the ultrasound. And this time the ultrasound was offered same day because now they could see something. Um, because it was so close to the surface of my skin now, and that's the only reason they could see it. There was just this very slight little change, very close to the surface of my skin. And they could go in and look at that very specific small little spot, um, with an ultrasound. And that did end up being, um, a calcification. The, they call it DCIS. Um. It's not spread anywhere, it's just small enough. It's still in the duct. Um, but it was different and it was new and it gave them the ability to, to get this approved to do the ultrasound. Um, but what I found out later is that. It. It's like going to a dermatologist looking for a mole on your body that you've been recommended, right? For skin cancer. They're gonna look at that one specific spot, so they only ultrasounded that area at that spot. I did not get a full breast ultrasound on both breasts is what I later found out. Um. Even though I had category four extremely dense breast throughout both breasts. So even with my family history, even with a new, um, calcification, even now they're seeing something, I still never got the full breast ultrasound on both breasts.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with a strong barrier signal (fragmented_care) detailing a specific failure in the diagnostic process within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: You know. At the time, they, they sent me for a biopsy, and things start moving so quickly that you barely have time to even think about how you feel. But you're gonna, you've, but you're now like in this mode where you've just gotta keep going and so you can't notice like you have to.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong emotional signals (overwhelm) directly linked to the speed and nature of the healthcare_provider interaction during diagnosis.

moment_text: Move quickly before it crumbles. Right. You kind of feel like you're chasing this bridge that's crumbling behind you and you're trying to just get to the other side of it. Um, it was scary just because I didn't know I was at work, you know? I was like, okay, that's nice. I gotta get back to work. Sorry. You know, and you're just. Uh, I literally just didn't know how I was going to deal with this or how I was gonna feel about it. I, you know, had to go pick up my kids from school and I had to, you know, just figure out the rest of my day and how I was going to fit this in. And I know that sounds really strange to say and super disconnected, but. That was my reality. I was already in survival mode and then add this on top of it. It just was, um, it felt impossible to figure out how to deal with it. Um, yeah.

moment_text: I will have to say that I was not super graceful in the beginning. I am. I really fought this. I am, like I said, I can be a little headstrong and I can also be very independent. And not that I don't want help and didn't need help, I just was. In such a mode that I was on such a, a tight schedule, I would, I would just say, I used to joke that it was the fire alarm that woke me up every day. I woke up with that sense of urgency every morning that like, I gotta get up and go. Um, and so having this really take me down and force me to, to sit down and think about. What was happening and ask for help. Even though I had it all around me, I hadn't allowed myself to really ask for it. Um, I guess I have a wonderful family. I have wonderful friends. I just, it was so foreign to me, um, to be able to just sit down and accept that this was going to change my life and I needed to, to believe that, and I needed to ask for help.

moment_text: Um, absolutely not. I will say thank goodness for the conversation I had with the radiologist. I think that at the time, that was the most impactful conversation I had, um, after I was diagnosed with breast cancer. I was sent for an exam. I don't honestly even remember what it was. Um, and then I was sent home with a stack of books and pamphlets about this tall that I, I, I wasn't gonna sit down and read like, I mean, it's nice, but you start thumbing through it and like, what does this mean? What am I supposed to do with this? It's a bunch of resources that don't feel super relevant right now. I want, I needed to make a choice. Um. Find a doctor I trusted to do a surgery on me. They were recommending a lumpectomy for me, and then five to 10 years of Tamoxifen. And just based on my mother's experience, I just knew that wasn't gonna be it for me.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: This moment contains multiple barrier signals (lack of information, lack of validation), making it eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: That was not the the path I needed to take nor the path that was gonna work for me or be successful for me, that I just knew very strongly in my gut and. That is what my care team kept driving me towards. It works great for some people. I just knew it wasn't my path, and so I went down a course of interviewing doctors, looking for a new care team, trying to work with the doctors I had that I had that were supposedly the best. There were. Everybody kept highly recommending this team. To me. These are the best. This is who you want. If I had this, this is who I would go see. Um. But the more I talked to them and the more I talked about what I felt I wanted, the more it became apparent that I didn't trust these people to, to treat me.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: This moment contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy, changing_providers) and barrier signals (provider_dismissal), making it eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: And I didn't want them to perform any surgeries on me. Um, and I learned a lot more about dense breast tissue in that time, and so I'm super grateful. I will always say this to Dr. Nancy Capello, who was the only woman I found at the time. From ru dense.org that was really driving home how this affects women, um, and their choices and their screening and our ability to detect cancer in dense breast tissue. Um, and so I eventually found an entirely new team, fired. My old team, found a whole new team that was willing to listen to what I wanted. Give me the double mastectomy. Um, offered me a reconstruction, which I desired and wanted. Um, and then I, yeah. I'm sorry to answer your question immediately. No. But when I found the right team, yes. And it was a lot of work, and I know that, that not every person has that amount of time to do that legwork or the resources to do that legwork. Um, and I had my family helping me. I had my whole family doing a bunch of research behind the scenes for me as well.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: This moment contains strong agency signals (seeking_information, changing_providers) and barrier signals (education_gap, access_barrier), making it eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: You know, in my heart of hearts, having a seen my mom, you know, who had wanted a double mastectomy to begin with, um. Was denied it and then many years later had breast cancer in her other breast and then was able to get, um. Her other breast, uh, of single mastectomy. But by this time, you know, she needed chemo. It had spread to her, to her lymph, and it was a much more aggressive treatment. Um, then compounded with the, the fact that understanding dense breast tissue was. Going to put me at higher risk for screening that I was going to meet, need more ultrasound or MRI, to be able to get my screenings and require more screenings than I was comfortable having and constantly be searching for something, um, around, you know, if. My, you know, obviously within my breast, but, you know, searching through the dense breast tissue and I thought, do I really wanna keep going for all these mammograms and MRIs? Um, or would I rather have a double mastectomy and also the Tamoxifen and everything else. I just having, again, having washed my mom's experience, I. I, I did not want that for myself. And so I found a team of doctors that understood what the screening process would've looked like for me had I decided to keep my breasts, um, and agreed that with the level of density in my breasts. That this was probably the wisest choice, that by the time we found something, again, I would not probably be this lucky again to find it as early as I had. And so to have a team that backed up that decision felt really good.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: This moment contains strong agency signals (making_decisions, self_advocacy) and a barrier signal (education_gap), making it eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: Um, I will say that after my surgery, my surgeon called me. And she asked me if I was sitting down, this was my breast surgeon. So I had two surgeons, the breast surgeon and the reconstructive plastic surgeon. Um, it was the breast surgeon that called me afterwards, um, to let me know that the pathology report was back. And she asked me if I was sitting down and I said, yes, except for I immediately jumped up and started pacing the floor. I'm like, what are you about to tell me? Um, and she said. You know when you find out you have breast cancer, I don't know if they did this to you, but they start calling one breast, the diseased breast, and the other breast, the healthy breast, which I was like, can we stop saying that? I don't love that. And so for me it was my right breast, what they called the diseased breast and my left breast that they called the healthy breast. And um, she said your left breast was loaded with breast cancer. And those were her exact words. She gave me no medical terminology loaded. She said, we saw so many tumors throughout your breast. We saw, um, atypical cells all throughout your breast. She said it was loaded. Thank goodness. Thank goodness you listened to what you knew you wanted. Thank goodness we gave you this double mastectomy.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: This is a powerful moment containing strong emotional signals (validation, relief), agency signals (self_advocacy), and barrier signals (misdiagnosis), making it highly eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: Yeah. This is such a hard question because I think everybody wants a clear answer and sometimes we don't get. A super clear answer, like I might never have gotten that phone call that validated the choice that I made. I just had to be comfortable with my choice no matter what. And I think at the end of the day when we're given some difficult choices. There's not really a guarantee either way. We do really need to find out the answer that's gonna let us rest as best as possible. At the end of the day, when we put our head on the pillow, what is going to offer me the most peace? What is gonna offer me the most safety or the most assurance that I can get? 'cause the reality is nothing is gonna be a hundred percent, but what is going to give me the most peace? And to me, that's what I come back to at the end of the day. So I love to do my research. I love to know what all my options are. I wanna look further down the road and play out the different scenarios. But at the end of the day, when I need to pick one, I pick the one that's gonna offer me the most peace.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: This moment contains agency signals (making_decisions, seeking_information), making it eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: Mm-hmm. I say that because my experience showed me so many gaps in the system as a. What I thought I was well informed, well educated, well supported, single mom was probably the only thing I didn't have going for me, but still had good health insurance and a white woman in the system. And, you know, all, all the access to the tools and resources I could need. I, at the end of the day, I did have gas in my car and I did have food on the table, right? So, um. But noticing these holes in the system and really feeling how hard it still felt for me and how alone I still felt. It really kept me up at night to think about what other women must be facing if they were going through this too.

moment_text: And when I say that the disparity happens before you even get to your mammogram appointment, um. A lot of people aren't even having these conversations in their households. Um, they don't even know where to start to get. This type of information, it's not being given to them in a way that is, um, with a sense of importance or urgency or in a way that maybe they can even interpret and understand, depending on if there's also a language barrier.

moment_text: We've had, we've, we've all had that drilled into us for so long now, this campaign of go get a mammogram, those are now pretty available, fairly easy to access. They're paid for. And so I started to see a huge gap, especially. In the, the way we're talking about dense breast tissue, but also in providing access to that care, and all of these things are compounded by socioeconomic factors. If you can even take another day off work. Put gas in your car, get another daycare center or a babysitter, another copay, and then paying for, for the extra screening, which was a choice I know I had to make.

moment_text: Yeah, absolutely. I think, um. First of all, there's only one way to diagnose dense breast tissue, and it is with a mammogram. So the only way to know that, and I've, I've, I've had women tell me, oh, I can feel my breasts. They're very denser. My doctor did an exam and told me they were, they were very dense and I said, they might feel that way, and you might also have dense breast tissue, but only a mammogram can diagnose. Dense breast tissue and tell you what your density type is.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate because it contains barrier signals (lack_of_information, education_gap) by correcting a common misconception within a healthcare context.

moment_text: And also we know that density can change throughout your lifetime. However, I think individualized care is essential and really important to look at what type of density do you have and is there just an area of density or do you have full breast density like I did? You might only need an area that gets ultrasound or you might need MRI, um, on full both breasts full. Breast, MRI or ultrasound. And so I think it's really important to have these conversations with your doctor. Right now, the standard of care is to a follow up ultrasound for stage three or stage four dense breast tissue. Um, and so again, if you have bigger concerns or stage one or two, um, that may mean that you have a small. Area of density, that area still may be very dense and require an ultrasound. So it is going to vary person to person. And I think there's a lot of nuance there. And this is where the self-advocacy piece, I think is going to play a key role in getting women the information to help understand what these. Terms mean and what it means for, for follow-up care.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate because it contains multiple agency signals (self_advocacy, speaking_up) and barrier signals (education_gap) related to navigating healthcare.

moment_text: Um, my wish and my dream would be that you get walked down the hall to the ultrasound, whether or not they can see anything and get that ultrasound the same day so you're not having to make another appointment. Pay another copay, take another day off work. Um, I would love to see a streamlined process, and that's what the Clear Pathways program is for. Beyond that, I see this as a real issue of delay of care. Most women are not coming back for that second appointment. Excuse me. And getting that second screening in a timely manner. And I would love to see that happen within a six week period so that we can actually say that we are doing our due diligence, uh, for early breast cancer detection. And that's why you'll hear me say a lot. Um, we believe everybody has the right to an early breast cancer diagnosis, and this delay of care is not affording everybody that privilege.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate because it contains multiple barrier signals (delayed_diagnosis, cost_constraint, fragmented_care) and an agency signal (speaking_up) within a healthcare context.

moment_text: I think that there's an awareness around it that wasn't before. Um, but I. Think everything else that you said is absolutely true. It's not being communicated, the level of importance of it that it is and the level of I, I use the word urgency, not because dense breast tissue is the problem itself, but the level of urgency. When I say that, I mean your mammogram. Isn't clear, we don't know is what that means. And so when you see something on there that says normal mammogram or no abnormal findings or no evidence of cancer, that's great. But the truth is we don't actually know until we go get an ultrasound or an MRI. And so I think there's a fault sense of security being communicated. And so that's why I say there's an. A level of importance or a level of urgency that's missing because a woman will think she's done her due diligence and gone and gotten her mammogram and thinks she's good for another year. And she might be, and hopefully that's the case. But the truth is, until we get that follow up screening, we can't give her that assurance. And so I, I don't wanna be given people this false sense of assurance when the truth is we don't know.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate because it contains barrier signals (lack_of_information, lack_of_validation) and emotional signals (frustration) related to a healthcare system interaction.

moment_text: I think this is absolutely a two-pronged AP approach. Um, one, the communication has to be there. The, the sense of importance needs to be there. My dream scenario in that case then is that if she also needs other resources, gas in the car, and, uh, a friend, a sister, a daycare provider, something right? So that, you know, when we book her for that follow up appointment, she's also being asked, is this doable for you? Will you be able to make it back? How? How difficult will that be to come back? For some women that is. Uh, insurmountable. So when you at least start asking the questions, you're having them recognize the importance of it and looking at how to problem solve some of the problem, the, the situations that they might come up against to, to ensure that they get an accurate screening. And I think that even just asking the questions will make a huge difference in, in having the woman feel empowered to come back. But in addition to that, also offering resources, should they be necessary?
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate because it contains both agency signals (asking_questions) and barrier signals (access_barrier, lack_of_support) within a healthcare context.

moment_text: I don't think so. I don't think it's being taken into account the way that it could be. Um, there's a lot of factors in there. Um, but, you know, somebody younger in that age bracket still might be planning to have kids or may be single and completely alone. You know, there's, there's so many other lifestyles that are happening. I think that they, they see somebody, um, in that age, age range and think that they probably have, you know, their. They look healthy and well. Right? They look a certain way, and so you think that they have, you know, certain access to things that they might not have access to. Track those gaps in care, see how we can facilitate getting the women back, um, and improve those numbers because I, I, I'll be sharing this very soon. moment_text: And I think when you take the time to, to follow through with that, because we all talk about it, but I mean, if we're being honest, are, are we all doing our monthly self-breast exam? Um, it's a powerful tool for tracking when things feel different for you. But the underlying thing that happens when you do your self breast exam is building a little bit of self-trust and self-confidence in yourself. And I think that goes a long way when you go to seek care from your care providers when you are also participating. In your care in a way that feels empowering and knowledgeable and that you've got yourself right. You're gonna have a lot of support. moment_text: Um, and then arming yourself with resources, you know. Checking up on, you know, the resources that you have available, the ones that we have available. The appropriate choices and feel like you can have informed conversations with your care providers. And if you're feeling dismissed, I think that's a red flag. If you can't find care providers that are willing to have these conversations, if you are able, then please seek out care providers that are willing to have these conversations. And if you're not, then that's what groups like. Yours is for what mine are for, to have those conversations from other people that have the experience. moment_text: Okay, so here we go. I, I pulled up the four questions because my memory is, you know, menopause. So, um, will my co insurance cover a 3D mammogram? Because sometimes that's an option and standard. Um, and don't confuse that for an ultrasound because sometimes they will tell you, well, it's a three DD mammogram and so it's better. And yes, it is better, but it's still a mammogram and you still need the ultrasound, so. Do, did you get a 3D mammogram? And will your insurance cover the ultrasound? Um, depending on what state you're in, they might, but you still might also advocate and have to push for insurance to deem it necessary. So there's still nuance there. Even if you are living in a state that has the, um, insurance coverage built in. Um, do I have dense breast tissue? Important question. What stage? Of dense breast tissue. moment_text: The third question is, is genetic screening right for me? Um, you may or may not already know the answer to this, but it's worth asking because. Screenings are changing all the time.
